title: Best drill bits for metal in 2026: Bits That Hold Up on Steel
excerpt: Find drill bit sets that actually stand up to metal, from mild steel to stainless, so you stop burning through bits on every hole.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Find drill bit sets that actually stand up to metal, from mild steel to stainless, so you stop burning through bits on every hole.
intro:
A drill bit set labeled for metal can inspire confidence, but after a few holes in mild steel the edges chip and the bit wanders. Most users blame the bit, but the real culprit is often a mismatch between the steel alloy, the coating, and the drilling technique.

We cut through the marketing fluff to highlight the sets that actually hold an edge under real metalworking conditions. From titanium-coated workhorses for everyday steel to genuine cobalt bits that power through stainless, our picks match specific use cases rather than chasing empty material claims.

Whether you’re drilling a bracket in the garage or punching holes in stainless sheet all day, the right bit — and knowing when to slow down and oil it — transforms a frustrating job into a clean, repeatable task.

products.title: DEWALT Drill Bit Set, 21 Pc. Titanium Nitride Coated Pilot Point DEWALT Bit Set with Case for Wood, Metal and Plastic (DW1361)
products.label: DEWALT DW1361 Titanium Set
products.badge: Best Overall for Metal & General Use
products.oneLiner: 21-piece titanium pilot-point set with hex shank for clean metal and wood holes.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Pair bits under 1/8" with cutting oil and slower RPM when drilling into metal to prevent snapping.
pros: Pilot point tips start true with less walking, reducing hole location errors in metal. — 379 mentions on functionality, 96% positive
No-spin hex shank eliminates chuck slip when drilling hard materials.
21 pieces span 1/16" to 1/2" with extra small size multiples, covering everyday metal fastening tasks. — 201 mentions on versatility, 89% positive
cons: Small-diameter bits may snap under high pressure without cutting oil, a tradeoff of HSS twist drills. — 538 mentions on durability, 37% negative — occasional breakage reported
products.verdict: For metal drilling that prioritizes clean starts and chuck grip across mild steel and everyday materials, the DW1361’s pilot point and hex shank design set it apart — just give small bits cutting oil and moderate speed.
products.review:
The DEWALT DW1361 set pairs titanium nitride-coated pilot point tips with a no-spin hex shank. The tips bite into mild steel, aluminum, and plastic with minimal skating, while the hex shank prevents rotation inside the chuck during high-torque metal work. Ranging from 1/16" to 1/2", the 21 bits include multiples of common sizes, so you’re not left hunting for a replacement when a small bit wears. Sharp edges hold up through repeated holes in varied materials, though the smallest diameters — like any HSS twist drill — can snap if pushed hard without cutting oil.

DIYers and professionals who drill mild steel, aluminum, wood, or plastic get a versatile set that balances cost and everyday capability. This is not a heavy-industrial set for hardened steel or production-line stainless; that territory belongs to cobalt bits. The honest tradeoff: small bits demand lubrication and moderate speed, which is standard for the category, not a unique failure point. Budget-first shoppers will find cheaper options, but they sacrifice the pilot point precision and hex shank grip that make this set work across so many common tasks.

💡 💡 Tip: Pair bits under 1/8" with cutting oil and slower RPM when drilling into metal to prevent snapping.

products.label: DEWALT DWA1184 HSS Set

products.verdict: For occasional metal drilling where a locked-in chuck matters more than exotic coatings, this set delivers DEWALT fundamentals at an easy entry price.
products.review:
The 3-flat shank design is the practical standout here, giving the chuck a positive grip that reduces spin-out during metal drilling. The high-speed steel bits start sharp and work cleanly across mild steel, aluminum, wood, and plastic. The compact case clips into other DEWALT storage, keeping a tidy bench.

The black and gold coating provides basic friction reduction, but edge life shortens on abrasive metals or prolonged stainless work compared to a titanium pilot-point set. This set suits homeowners and hobbyists who drill occasional metal holes and value a secure chuck hold over premium coatings. Heavy continuous use on stainless or hardened plate calls for a cobalt upgrade.

💡 💡 Tip: Keep RPM low and use cutting oil on metal — the black oxide finish lasts longer when heat stays under control.

products.title: BOSCH CO14B 14-Piece Assorted Set with Included Case - Cobalt M42 Metal Drill Bits with Three-Flat Shank for Drilling Applications in Stainless Steel, Cast Iron, Titanium, and Light-Gauge Metal
products.label: Bosch CO14B Cobalt Set
products.oneLiner: 14-piece M42 cobalt set with split point for high-heat stainless and cast iron drilling.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Always use cutting oil and stay below 500 RPM when drilling stainless to prevent snapping.
pros: Genuine M42 cobalt alloy delivers reliable edge retention on stainless steel and cast iron. — 339 mentions on quality, 89% positive
Heat resistance extends bit life significantly beyond standard HSS. — Multiple reviews mention 10x life claim, sharpness holds
Sharp 135° split point starts clean holes without walking. — 140 mentions on sharpness, 70% positive
cons: Some stainless steel grades may resist these bits more than the packaging suggests. — 44 mentions on material compatibility, 41% negative
Brittle cobalt can snap under high RPM or dry drilling — demands careful speed and lubrication. — 193 mentions on durability, 41% negative — many breakage reports, often due to user error
products.verdict: A purposeful choice for metal fabricators who match cobalt's demands with proper speed and lubrication — not a grab-and-go set for mixed-material projects.
products.review:
Where the top pick Dewalt set handles mild steel with a titanium pilot point, the Bosch CO14B is purpose-made for the friction and heat of stainless steel, hardened alloys, and cast iron. The M42 cobalt alloy maintains sharpness far longer than standard HSS in tough metals, but it requires careful technique: low RPM, steady pressure, and cutting oil are non-negotiable. Without that, these brittle bits can snap, making them a poor fit for casual DIY or wood projects. This set suits metalworkers who regularly drill hard materials and understand the necessary speeds and lubrication.

💡 💡 Tip: Always use cutting oil and stay below 500 RPM when drilling stainless to prevent snapping.

pros: USA-made hi-molybdenum steel with gold oxide cuts hardened steel cleanly — 171 mentions, 95% positive on quality
135° split point eliminates walking and reduces feed pressure — 52 mentions, 92% positive on sharpness
Holds an edge longer than standard HSS bits in production metalwork — 52 mentions, 71% positive on durability
products.verdict: For daily metalwork where hardened steel is routine, this set's longevity and cutting ability make the premium price a sound investment.
products.review:
Hi-Molybdenum steel with gold oxide coating stays sharp through hardened steel and thick plate. The 29-piece set covers 1/16 in. to 1/2 in. by 64ths, and the 135° split point starts holes cleanly without wandering. While the top pick adds versatility with a hex shank and pilot point, this set is for when you need bits that won't quit in tool steel or stainless.

For machinists, fabricators, and anyone drilling metal daily, the premium price pays off in longevity and accuracy. Straight shank may spin under high torque in some chucks, so it pairs best with a drill press or a heavy-duty keyed chuck. Occasional drillers and budget-focused DIYers won't see the return.

products.label: Bosch TI5IM Impact Set

products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Let the impact do the work — apply light pressure to avoid snapping the smallest bits.

products.verdict: A specialized 5-piece set for mechanics and installers who need impact-driver convenience in tight spaces; not a replacement for a deep drill index.
products.review:
Unlike cobalt sets built for drill presses, this 5-piece Bosch kit pairs an impact-rated hex shank with a no-skate titanium tip — so you can pop clean 3/32' to 1/4' holes in steel using an impact driver, even around engine bays and machinery enclosures. The bits start fast without a center punch and cut quickly through sheet metal and brackets. The limiting factor is working length: these are stubby bits that only clear shallow material. They fill a narrow role — quick, out-of-position holes where a full-length drill index won't fit. For deeper holes or heavy plate, a longer cobalt set is the smarter call.

💡 💡 Tip: Let the impact do the work — apply light pressure to avoid snapping the smallest bits.

buyingGuide.heading: Material
buyingGuide.body:
High Speed Steel (HSS) is the baseline. It handles mild steel, aluminum, and wood with ease, and adding a titanium or black oxide coating reduces friction. For occasional metal repair, HSS is the economical choice.

Cobalt alloys (M42 with 8% cobalt) retain hardness at high temperatures, making them essential for stainless steel, cast iron, and titanium. But cobalt is brittle — running it at high RPM or without cutting oil leads to snapping. Low-end 'cobalt' sets often use weaker M35 alloy or inconsistent heat treatment and fail quickly.

buyingGuide.heading: Point Style
buyingGuide.body:
Split point and pilot point tips center themselves on the metal surface, reducing walking and the need for a center punch. A 135° split point cuts with less pressure than a standard 118° point, which is a real advantage when drilling overhead or in tight spots.

Pilot points add a secondary guiding tip that excels in sheet metal and aluminum, though they can clog in gummy materials. Standard points require a firm punch mark but are easiest to sharpen, so they still have a place in well-equipped shops.

buyingGuide.heading: Coating
buyingGuide.body:
A coating reduces friction and can extend bit life, but it’s the steel underneath that does the real work. Titanium nitride coatings are durable and effective on HSS bits; black oxide adds lubricity and is common on mid-range sets.

Gold oxide on high carbon steel is largely cosmetic and wears off quickly in metal. Don’t choose a set based on coating alone — a quality uncoated cobalt bit will outlast a heavily coated low-grade HSS bit every time.

buyingGuide.heading: Size Range
buyingGuide.body:
A set that covers 1/16" to 1/2" in 1/64" increments gives you flexibility for bolt holes, pilot holes, and clearance holes. Many 14-piece sets skip the 1/2" bit entirely, even when the packaging or listing photos suggest otherwise.

If your work involves mounting brackets or running bolts larger than 3/8", verify the set actually includes a 1/2" bit before buying. For metric projects, a 1mm–10mm set with 0.5mm steps serves a similar role.

buyingGuide.heading: Shank Type
buyingGuide.body:
A 3-flat shank resists spin-out in standard chucks, a common frustration when drilling tough materials. Hex shanks lock into impact drivers for fast work but limit drilling depth and are rare in sizes above 1/4".

Straight round shanks are the most universal and sharpenable, but they demand a well-tightened chuck. If you rely on an impact driver for metal, a dedicated hex-shank set is worth the limited size range.

faq.question: Why do my titanium drill bits dull quickly when drilling steel?
faq.answer: High heat is usually the culprit. Titanium nitride coating reduces friction, but if the bit is run at high RPM or without cutting oil, the tip overheats and loses its edge. Use a lower speed — 500–700 RPM for 1/4" bits in mild steel — and apply a drop of oil to keep the cutting edge cool.

faq.question: Can I use regular HSS drill bits for stainless steel?
faq.answer: Standard HSS bits will burn up or glaze over on stainless steel within a few holes. The work-hardening properties of stainless demand cobalt or carbide bits that maintain hardness at high temperatures. If you must use HSS, go extremely slow, use heavy lubrication, and expect a short life.

faq.question: What speed should I drill metal with cobalt bits?
faq.answer: Cobalt bits transfer heat away from the tip best at low speeds. For stainless steel, run around 300–500 RPM for a 1/4" bit, and always use cutting oil. Going above 800 RPM risks instantly snapping the bit or work-hardening the stock.

faq.question: Do I need a pilot hole when drilling metal with split point bits?
faq.answer: A split point bit is designed to start on contact without walking, so a pilot hole is usually unnecessary for holes under 1/2". However, on curved or slick surfaces like pipe, a small center punch mark still helps the bit bite cleanly.
